1:
"And I saw an angel come down from heaven, having the key of the bottomless pit and a great chain in his hand." The brief verse notes the angel with the key of the abyss and a great chain. The grammatical voices note the descending angel. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "an angel come down from heaven, having the key of the bottomless pit (the abyss) and a great chain in his hand" — an angel descends with the key of the abyss and a great chain, to bind and imprison the dragon (vv.2-3); the instruments mark his commission to restrain Satan.

On "the key of the bottomless pit," the witnesses note his authority over the abyss. Barnes and Ellicott note that the angel holds "the key of the bottomless pit" — authority to open and shut the abyss; the same abyss from which the locusts and beast arose (9:1; 11:7); here the prison into which Satan is cast.

On "a great chain in his hand," the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede and the grammatical-historical note the binding-chain: the angel has "a great chain" to bind the dragon; the figure of the restraining, binding of Satan; Victorinus and Bede read the binding as the restraint of the devil's power, in the framework of the thousand years (vv.2-7); the great chain signifies the mighty restraint laid on Satan. The verse displays the angel with the key of the abyss and a great chain.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the angel — "an angel come down from heaven, having the key of the bottomless pit and a great chain in his hand," the angel commissioned to bind and imprison Satan (Barnes, Robertson); "the key of the bottomless pit" his authority over the abyss (9:1; 11:7, Barnes, Ellicott); "a great chain" the instrument of binding Satan (Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the binding-angel. The verse marks the angel: from heaven. The verse displays the key: of the abyss. The verse displays the chain: great. The verse displays the commission: to bind Satan. The angel displays the power to restrain the dragon. The verse displays the angel: descending. The verse displays the key: held. The verse displays the chain: ready. The angel with the key of the abyss and a great chain displays the opening of the millennial vision: an angel comes down with the key of the bottomless pit and a great chain, commissioned to bind and imprison the dragon. The verse displays the binding-angel, who seizes the dragon (v.2).
2:
"And he laid hold on the dragon, that old serpent, which is the Devil, and Satan, and bound him a thousand years," The brief verse notes the binding of Satan for a thousand years. On "he laid hold on the dragon, that old serpent, which is the Devil, and Satan," the grammatical voices note the seizing of the adversary. Barnes and Robertson note the capture: "he laid hold on the dragon, that old serpent, which is the Devil, and Satan" — the angel seizes the dragon, identified (as 12:9) as the old serpent, the Devil, and Satan; the great adversary is laid hold of and bound.

On "bound him a thousand years," interpreters note the binding and the period. The grammatical-historical Barnes and the spiritual/amillennial reading (Bede, after Augustine) take the binding as the restraint of Satan's power during a long period — the gospel age or a coming era — in which his deceiving of the nations is curbed (v.3); the "thousand years" taken symbolically of a long, complete period. The premillennial/chiliast reading (Gill, Victorinus, the futurist) takes the binding as a future imprisonment of Satan for a literal thousand years, during which Christ and the risen saints reign on earth (vv.4-6); the literal millennium.

On "a thousand years," the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede note the period: the "thousand years" is read by Bede (amillennial) as a symbol of the gospel age (the present reign of Christ and his church), and by Victorinus (chiliast) as a coming era; the binding curbs Satan's power for the appointed time. The verse displays the binding of Satan for a thousand years.

The witnesses agree the substance: the angel seizes the dragon (the Devil, Satan) and binds him for a thousand years, restraining him (Barnes, Robertson, Victorinus, Bede). The nature of the binding and the thousand years is part of the great millennial crux (see v.4): a symbolic restraint during the gospel age (amillennial — Bede), or a future literal imprisonment during a literal millennium (premillennial — Gill, Victorinus). The verse displays the bound Satan. The verse marks the dragon: seized. The verse displays the identity: the Devil, Satan. The verse displays the binding: a thousand years. The verse displays the frame: the millennial crux. The capture displays the adversary bound for the appointed period. The verse displays the dragon: laid hold of. The verse displays the Satan: bound. The verse displays the years: a thousand. The binding of Satan for a thousand years displays the restraint of the adversary: the angel lays hold of the dragon — the old serpent, the Devil, Satan — and binds him a thousand years; read by some as the restraint of Satan during the gospel age, by others as a future literal imprisonment. The verse displays the bound Satan, sealed in the abyss (v.3).
3:
"And cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up, and set a seal upon him, that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled: and after that he must be loosed a little season." The witnesses note Satan sealed in the abyss, to deceive no more for the thousand years. On "cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up, and set a seal upon him," the grammatical voices note the imprisonment. Barnes and Robertson note the secure confinement: "cast him into the bottomless pit (the abyss), and shut him up, and set a seal upon him" — Satan is cast into the abyss, shut up, and sealed; the threefold action (cast, shut, sealed) marks his secure, complete imprisonment; he is wholly restrained.

On "that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Ellicott note that the binding is "that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled" — the purpose is to stop Satan's deception of the nations for the period; in the amillennial reading, the restraint of his deceiving power during the gospel age; in the premillennial, his removal during the literal millennium; the nations are freed from his deception for the thousand years.

On "after that he must be loosed a little season," the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede and the grammatical-historical note the loosing: "after that he must be loosed a little season" — when the thousand years end, Satan is released for a short time (vv.7-9), to make a final assault before his final doom; the binding is not yet his final destruction; a brief loosing precedes the end. The verse displays Satan sealed in the abyss, to deceive no more for the thousand years.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the imprisonment — "cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up, and set a seal upon him," Satan securely confined and sealed (Barnes, Robertson); "that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled," the restraint of his deception for the period (Barnes, Ellicott); "after that he must be loosed a little season," the brief final loosing before the end (vv.7-9, Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the sealed Satan. The verse marks the pit: shut. The verse displays the seal: set. The verse displays the purpose: deceive no more. The verse displays the loosing: a little season. The imprisonment displays Satan bound from deceiving the nations for the period. The verse displays the abyss: sealed. The verse displays the deception: halted. The verse displays the loosing: brief. Satan sealed in the abyss, to deceive no more for the thousand years, displays the restraint of the adversary: he is cast into the abyss, shut up, and sealed, that he deceive the nations no more till the thousand years are fulfilled — and after that he must be loosed a little season. The verse displays the bound Satan, with the reign of the saints (vv.4-6).
4:
"And I saw thrones, and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them: and I saw the souls of them that were beheaded for the witness of Jesus, and for the word of God, and which had not worshipped the beast, neither his image, neither had received his mark upon their foreheads, or in their hands; and they lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years." The witnesses note the martyrs reigning with Christ a thousand years — the central crux of the millennium. On "I saw thrones, and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them," the grammatical voices note the enthroned saints. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "I saw thrones, and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them" — thrones are set, and those on them are given authority to reign/judge; the saints share in Christ's rule.

On "the souls of them that were beheaded for the witness of Jesus... and they lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years," interpreters divide over the reign and the resurrection. The premillennial/chiliast reading (the Reformed Gill, the latin-fathers Victorinus, the futurist) takes it literally: the martyrs and saints are bodily raised (the first resurrection, v.5) and reign with Christ on earth for a literal thousand years. The grammatical-historical Barnes takes the "living and reigning" figuratively — not a bodily resurrection but the revival of the spirit and cause of the martyrs, true religion becoming the reigning, pervading spirit on earth (a revival/postmillennial reading). The amillennial reading (Bede, after Augustine; Ellicott notes it) takes the souls of the martyrs as living and reigning with Christ in heaven during the present gospel age, the "first resurrection" being spiritual.

On "which had not worshipped the beast, neither his image," the puritan Henry notes the faithful: those who reign are the martyrs and the faithful who refused the beast and his mark (ch. 13); the reign is the reward of the faithful witnesses. The verse displays the martyrs reigning with Christ a thousand years.

The witnesses agree the substance: the souls of the martyrs (who refused the beast) live and reign with Christ a thousand years (Barnes, Gill, Victorinus, Bede, Henry). But the nature of the reign and the "first resurrection" (v.5) is the great millennial crux: a literal bodily resurrection and earthly reign of the saints with Christ for a literal thousand years (premillennial — Gill, Victorinus); the souls of the martyrs reigning with Christ in heaven during the present gospel age, the resurrection spiritual (amillennial — Bede/Augustine); or the figurative revival of the martyrs' cause and the reign of true religion on earth (Barnes). The verse displays the millennial crux. The verse marks the thrones: set. The verse displays the martyrs: reigning. The verse displays the debate: literal or spiritual reign. The verse displays the period: a thousand years. The vision displays the saints reigning with Christ for the appointed age. The verse displays the souls: enthroned. The verse displays the reign: with Christ. The verse displays the years: a thousand. The martyrs reigning with Christ a thousand years display the heart of the millennial vision and crux: the souls of the martyrs who refused the beast live and reign with Christ a thousand years — read by the premillennialists as a literal bodily resurrection and earthly reign (Gill, Victorinus), by the amillennialists as the martyrs reigning in heaven during the gospel age (Bede), and by Barnes as the figurative revival of their cause. The verse displays the reigning saints, in the first resurrection (vv.5-6).
5:
"But the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection." The witnesses note the first resurrection and the rest of the dead. On "the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished," the grammatical voices note the distinction. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: "the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished" — over against those who reigned (v.4), the rest of the dead did not live again until the thousand years ended; a distinction is drawn between the reigning saints and the rest, whose living again is delayed.

On "the rest of the dead lived not again," interpreters note the meaning. The premillennial reading (Gill, Victorinus) takes it of a literal distinction between two bodily resurrections — the saints raised first (the first resurrection), the rest (the wicked) raised at the end (the general resurrection, vv.12-13); the amillennial reading (Bede; Ellicott) takes the "living again" of the rest as their bodily resurrection at the last day, while the first resurrection (of the reigning saints) is spiritual; the two readings differ on whether two bodily resurrections, or one bodily and one spiritual.

On "This is the first resurrection," the grammatical-historical and Reformed note the key phrase: "This is the first resurrection" — the reigning of the saints (v.4) is called the first resurrection, in distinction from the second (the general resurrection of all the dead, v.12); whether this first resurrection is literal/bodily (premillennial — Gill) or spiritual/figurative (amillennial — Bede; or Barnes's revival), is the heart of the millennial debate (v.4). The verse displays the first resurrection and the rest of the dead.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the distinction — "the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection," the reigning saints distinguished from the rest, their reign called the first resurrection (Barnes, Robertson, Gill). The nature of the first resurrection is the millennial crux (v.4): literal/bodily (premillennial — Gill, Victorinus) vs spiritual (amillennial — Bede; or figurative revival — Barnes). The verse displays the first resurrection. The verse marks the rest: lived not yet. The verse displays the saints: first raised. The verse displays the phrase: the first resurrection. The verse displays the debate: literal or spiritual. The distinction displays the reigning saints in the first resurrection. The verse displays the rest: delayed. The verse displays the first: resurrection. The verse displays the crux: its nature. The first resurrection and the rest of the dead display the distinction central to the millennium: the rest of the dead did not live again until the thousand years ended — this is the first resurrection; the reigning of the saints, read as a literal bodily resurrection (premillennial) or a spiritual one (amillennial), the heart of the debate. The verse displays the first resurrection, with the beatitude on its partakers (v.6).
6:
"Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power, but they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years." The witnesses note the blessing on those in the first resurrection. On "Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection," the grammatical voices note the beatitude. Barnes and Robertson note the blessing: "Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection" — those who share in the first resurrection (v.5) are pronounced blessed and holy; the fifth of the seven beatitudes of Revelation; to have part in the first resurrection is blessedness and holiness.

On "on such the second death hath no power," the witnesses note the security. Barnes and Ellicott note that "on such the second death hath no power" — the second death (the lake of fire, v.14; 21:8) has no power over those in the first resurrection; they are secure from eternal death; their salvation is sure.

On "they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years," the grammatical-historical and Reformed note the priestly reign: they "shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years" — the partakers of the first resurrection are priests of God and Christ (1:6; 5:10) and reign with him; whether in the literal millennial reign (premillennial — Gill) or the present/heavenly reign (amillennial — Bede) or the reign of revived religion (Barnes); their blessed, priestly, royal estate with Christ. The verse displays the blessing on those in the first resurrection.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the beatitude — "Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection," the blessing on the partakers (Barnes, Robertson); "on such the second death hath no power," their security from eternal death (v.14; 21:8, Barnes, Ellicott); "they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years," their priestly, royal estate (1:6; 5:10, grammatical-historical, Gill). The verse displays the blessed partakers. The verse marks the blessing: blessed and holy. The verse displays the security: no second death. The verse displays the office: priests of God. The verse displays the reign: a thousand years. The beatitude displays the partakers blessed, secure, priestly, and reigning. The verse displays the partakers: blessed. The verse displays the death: powerless. The verse displays the priests: reigning. The blessing on those in the first resurrection displays the fifth beatitude: blessed and holy is he who has part in the first resurrection — the second death has no power over them, and they shall be priests of God and Christ and reign with him a thousand years. The verse displays the reigning priests, until Satan is loosed (vv.7-8).
7:
"And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison," The very brief verse notes Satan loosed at the end of the thousand years. The grammatical voices note the release. Barnes and Robertson note the loosing: "when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison" — at the end of the thousand years, Satan is released from his imprisonment (v.3); the binding was for the period only; his loosing precedes the final assault and his final doom (vv.8-10).

On "the thousand years are expired," the witnesses note the end of the period. Barnes and Henry note that "the thousand years are expired" — the millennial period (whether the gospel age or a literal millennium) comes to its end; the time of Satan's restraint is over.

On "Satan shall be loosed out of his prison," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the release: "Satan shall be loosed out of his prison" — Satan is freed for "a little season" (v.3) to make a last deception and assault; the loosing tests and reveals, and leads to his final destruction; the brief, final outbreak of evil before the end. The verse displays Satan loosed at the end of the thousand years.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the release — "when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison," Satan freed at the end of the period for the final assault (v.3, Barnes, Robertson); "the thousand years are expired," the millennial period ended (Barnes, Henry); "Satan shall be loosed out of his prison," the brief final loosing before his doom (Henry). The verse displays the loosed Satan. The verse marks the period: expired. The verse displays the release: from prison. The verse displays the purpose: final assault. The verse displays the sequel: his doom. The loosing displays Satan freed for the last outbreak. The verse displays the years: ended. The verse displays the Satan: loosed. The verse displays the season: brief. Satan loosed at the end of the thousand years displays the release of the adversary: when the thousand years are finished, Satan is loosed from his prison — freed for a little season to make his final deception and assault. The verse displays the loosed Satan, deceiving the nations Gog and Magog (v.8).
8:
"And shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them together to battle: the number of whom is as the sand of the sea." The witnesses note Satan gathering Gog and Magog to the final battle. On "shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth," the grammatical voices note the renewed deception. Barnes and Robertson note the loosed Satan's work: "shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth" — released, Satan resumes his deceiving of the nations (which the binding had stopped, v.3), gathering them from the four corners of the earth; the worldwide, final deception.

On "Gog and Magog," interpreters note the figure. Barnes and Ellicott note that "Gog and Magog" (drawn from Ezekiel 38-39, the great enemy from the north) is the symbolic name for the final, vast host of the enemies of God; whether literal future nations or a symbol of the gathered powers of evil in the last assault; the figure of the comprehensive, final hostility against God's people.

On "to gather them together to battle: the number of whom is as the sand of the sea," the grammatical-historical note the innumerable host: Satan gathers them "to battle: the number of whom is as the sand of the sea" — an innumerable host (Gog and Magog) is mustered for the final battle against the saints; the vast multitude (as the sand of the sea) marks the magnitude of the last assault; the final, futile war against God's people. The verse displays Satan gathering Gog and Magog to the final battle.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deception — "shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth," the loosed Satan resuming his worldwide deception (v.3, Barnes, Robertson); "Gog and Magog" the symbolic final host of God's enemies (Ezekiel 38-39, Barnes, Ellicott); "to gather them together to battle: the number... as the sand of the sea," the innumerable host mustered for the final assault (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered host. The verse marks the deception: of the nations. The verse displays the host: Gog and Magog. The verse displays the gathering: to battle. The verse displays the number: as the sand. The deception displays Satan mustering the final host against the saints. The verse displays the nations: deceived. The verse displays the Gog and Magog: gathered. The verse displays the host: innumerable. Satan gathering Gog and Magog to the final battle displays the last assault: the loosed Satan deceives the nations in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog, gathering them to battle, a host as the sand of the sea — the final, innumerable muster against God's people. The verse displays the gathered host, devoured by fire from heaven (v.9).
9:
"And they went up on the breadth of the earth, and compassed the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city: and fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them." The witnesses note the assault on the saints destroyed by fire from heaven. On "they went up on the breadth of the earth, and compassed the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city," the grammatical voices note the siege. Barnes and Robertson note the assault: "they went up on the breadth of the earth, and compassed the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city" — the host (Gog and Magog) spread over the earth and surrounded the camp of the saints and the beloved city (the church, or Jerusalem as the figure of God's people); the final siege of God's people by the gathered enemies.

On "the camp of the saints... and the beloved city," the witnesses note God's people. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the camp of the saints... and the beloved city" is the church — God's beloved people, gathered as in a camp or holy city; the object of the final assault; the figure of the people of God besieged.

On "fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them," the grammatical-historical note the sudden destruction: "fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them" — before any battle, fire from heaven devours the besieging host (as 2 Kings 1:10; Ezekiel 38:22; 39:6); the final assault is crushed not by the saints' arms but by God's direct judgment; the sudden, total destruction of the last enemies. The verse displays the assault on the saints destroyed by fire from heaven.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the siege — "they went up on the breadth of the earth, and compassed the camp of the saints about, and the beloved city," the final host surrounding God's people (Barnes, Robertson); "the camp of the saints... and the beloved city" the church, God's beloved people, besieged (Barnes, Ellicott); "fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them," the besiegers destroyed by God's fire (2 Kings 1:10; Ezekiel 38:22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the crushed assault. The verse marks the host: compassing the saints. The verse displays the city: beloved. The verse displays the fire: from heaven. The verse displays the destruction: devoured. The judgment displays the final enemies crushed by God's fire. The verse displays the saints: besieged. The verse displays the fire: descending. The verse displays the host: devoured. The assault on the saints destroyed by fire from heaven displays the crushing of the last enemy: the host surrounds the camp of the saints and the beloved city, but fire comes down from God and devours them — the final assault crushed by God's direct judgment, not the saints' arms. The verse displays the devoured host, with the devil cast into the lake of fire (v.10).
10:
"And the devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are, and shall be tormented day and night for ever and ever." The witnesses note the devil cast into the lake of fire forever. On "the devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone," the grammatical voices note Satan's final doom. Barnes and Robertson note the judgment: "the devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are" — Satan, the deceiver, is at last cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and false prophet were already cast (19:20); the unholy trinity meets its final, common doom; the devil's everlasting destruction.

On "where the beast and the false prophet are," the witnesses note the shared doom. Barnes and Gill note that Satan is cast "where the beast and the false prophet are" — joining the beast and false prophet (19:20) in the lake of fire; the three are united in their final punishment; the complete overthrow of the antichristian powers and their author.

On "shall be tormented day and night for ever and ever," the grammatical-historical note the eternal torment: they "shall be tormented day and night for ever and ever" — the punishment is unceasing and everlasting; the devil, beast, and false prophet suffer eternal torment; the final, irreversible doom of the powers of evil; the certainty and perpetuity of their judgment. The verse displays the devil cast into the lake of fire forever.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the doom — "the devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are," Satan's final destruction with the beast and false prophet (19:20, Barnes, Robertson); "where the beast and the false prophet are," the shared doom of the unholy trinity (Barnes, Gill); "shall be tormented day and night for ever and ever," the eternal, unceasing torment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the doomed devil. The verse marks the devil: cast down. The verse displays the lake: of fire. The verse displays the company: beast and false prophet. The verse displays the torment: forever. The doom displays Satan and his powers in eternal punishment. The verse displays the deceiver: cast. The verse displays the fire: eternal. The verse displays the torment: unceasing. The devil cast into the lake of fire forever displays the final doom of the adversary: the devil who deceived the nations is cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and false prophet are, to be tormented day and night forever and ever — the everlasting destruction of the powers of evil. The verse displays the doomed devil, before the great white throne (v.11).
11:
"And I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it, from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them." The witnesses note the great white throne of final judgment. On "I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it," the grammatical voices note the judgment-throne. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it" — a great white throne appears, with one seated on it (God, or Christ as judge); the white marks the purity and justice of the judgment; the throne of the final, general judgment of all the dead (vv.12-15).

On "a great white throne," the witnesses note its majesty and purity. Barnes and Henry note that the "great white throne" is the throne of judgment — great in majesty, white in spotless purity and justice; the seat of the final, righteous judgment of the world.

On "from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the dissolution: "from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them" — before the face of the Judge, the present earth and heaven flee away and vanish (cf. 21:1; 2 Peter 3:10); the old creation dissolves or is removed at the final judgment; the awful majesty of the Judge before whom the cosmos cannot stand. The verse displays the great white throne of final judgment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the throne — "I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it," the throne of the final, righteous judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "a great white throne" great in majesty, white in purity and justice (Barnes, Henry); "from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away," the old creation dissolving before the Judge (21:1; 2 Peter 3:10, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the judgment-throne. The verse marks the throne: great, white. The verse displays the judge: seated. The verse displays the dissolution: earth and heaven flee. The verse displays the majesty: awful. The vision displays the throne of final judgment before which the cosmos flees. The verse displays the throne: white. The verse displays the judge: enthroned. The verse displays the heaven: fled. The great white throne of final judgment displays the scene of the last judgment: a great white throne appears, with the Judge seated on it, from whose face the earth and heaven flee away, finding no place — the awful throne of the final, righteous judgment of all. The verse displays the throne, before which the dead are judged (vv.12-13).
12:
"And I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life: and the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works." The witnesses note the dead judged out of the books according to their works. On "I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God," the grammatical voices note the universal judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the scene: "I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God" — all the dead, of every rank (small and great), stand before God for judgment; the general, universal judgment of all mankind; none is exempt.

On "the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life," the witnesses note the books. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the books were opened" (the record of men's deeds) "and another book... which is the book of life" (the register of the saved) — two kinds of books: the books recording works, and the book of life recording the redeemed; the judgment proceeds from both.

On "the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the standard of judgment: "the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works" — the dead are judged according to their works, as recorded in the books; the principle of judgment by works (Romans 2:6; Matthew 16:27); each is judged by what he has done, the books bearing witness. The verse displays the dead judged out of the books according to their works.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the judgment — "I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened... and the dead were judged... according to their works," all the dead judged by their works from the books (Barnes, Robertson); "the books... and another book... which is the book of life," the record of works and the register of the saved (Barnes, Ellicott); "judged... according to their works," the principle of judgment by works (Romans 2:6, Cyril). The verse displays the final judgment. The verse marks the dead: small and great. The verse displays the books: opened. The verse displays the book of life: opened. The verse displays the standard: their works. The judgment displays all the dead judged by their works from the books. The verse displays the dead: standing. The verse displays the books: of record. The verse displays the works: judged. The dead judged out of the books according to their works display the great assize: all the dead, small and great, stand before God; the books are opened, and the book of life, and the dead are judged according to their works — the universal judgment by the record of deeds. The verse displays the judged dead, given up by sea, death, and hell (v.13).
13:
"And the sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and hell delivered up the dead which were in them: and they were judged every man according to their works." The brief verse notes the sea, death, and hell giving up their dead for judgment. The grammatical voices note the universal resurrection. Barnes and Robertson note the giving up of the dead: "the sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and hell (hades) delivered up the dead which were in them" — the sea, death, and hades surrender all their dead; all the dead, wherever they died or were held, are brought forth for judgment; the comprehensive, universal resurrection of all the dead to judgment.

On "the sea gave up the dead... and death and hell delivered up the dead," the witnesses note the comprehensiveness. Barnes and Gill note that "the sea... and death and hell (hades)" — every realm that held the dead (the sea, the grave, the abode of departed spirits) yields them up; none is missing; all are summoned to the judgment.

On "they were judged every man according to their works," the grammatical-historical note the standard: "they were judged every man according to their works" — each is judged individually by his works (v.12; Romans 2:6); the repetition stresses the principle of judgment by deeds; every man receives according to what he has done. The verse displays the sea, death, and hell giving up their dead for judgment.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the resurrection — "the sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and hell delivered up the dead which were in them," the comprehensive giving up of all the dead for judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "the sea... and death and hell," every realm yielding its dead (Barnes, Gill); "judged every man according to their works," each judged by his deeds (v.12; Romans 2:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the universal resurrection. The verse marks the sea: gave up its dead. The verse displays death and hell: delivered up. The verse displays the judgment: every man. The verse displays the standard: their works. The resurrection displays all the dead brought forth and judged. The verse displays the dead: surrendered. The verse displays the realms: emptied. The verse displays the works: judged. The sea, death, and hell giving up their dead for judgment display the universal resurrection to judgment: the sea, death, and hades surrender all their dead, and every man is judged according to his works — none missing from the final assize. The verse displays the judged dead, with death and hell cast into the lake of fire (v.14).
14:
"And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death." The very brief verse notes death and hell cast into the lake of fire, the second death. The grammatical voices note the abolition of death. Barnes and Robertson note the casting down: "death and hell (hades) were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death" — death and hades themselves are cast into the lake of fire; death, the last enemy, is abolished (1 Corinthians 15:26); the very powers of death and the grave are destroyed; the lake of fire is "the second death."

On "death and hell were cast into the lake of fire," the witnesses note the destruction of death. Barnes and Gill note that "death and hell (hades) were cast into the lake of fire" — death and the grave, having given up their dead, are themselves destroyed; the abolition of death and hades marks the end of their reign; no more death for the redeemed (21:4).

On "This is the second death," the grammatical-historical note the definition: "This is the second death" — the lake of fire is defined as the second death (cf. 21:8; 2:11; 20:6); the final, eternal death, as distinct from the first (bodily) death; the second death is the eternal perdition of the lost in the lake of fire. The verse displays death and hell cast into the lake of fire, the second death.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the abolition — "death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death," death and hades destroyed in the lake of fire (1 Corinthians 15:26, Barnes, Robertson); "death and hell... cast into the lake of fire," the end of death's reign (21:4, Barnes, Gill); "This is the second death," the lake of fire defined as the final, eternal death (21:8; 20:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the abolished death. The verse marks death and hell: cast down. The verse displays the lake: of fire. The verse displays the definition: the second death. The verse displays the end: of death's reign. The casting displays death itself destroyed in the lake of fire. The verse displays the death: abolished. The verse displays the hades: destroyed. The verse displays the second death: defined. Death and hell cast into the lake of fire, the second death, display the abolition of death: death and hades are cast into the lake of fire — this is the second death; the last enemy destroyed, the lake of fire the final, eternal death. The verse displays the second death, into which the unsaved are cast (v.15).
15:
"And whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire." The very brief verse notes the doom of those not in the book of life. The grammatical voices note the final sentence. Barnes and Robertson note the judgment: "whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire" — all whose names are not written in the book of life (v.12; 13:8) are cast into the lake of fire; the final sentence on the lost; the book of life is the decisive register, and those not found in it share the second death.

On "not found written in the book of life," the witnesses note the decisive register. Barnes and Gill note that "not found written in the book of life" — the book of life (the register of the redeemed) is the test; whoever is not recorded in it is condemned; the absence of one's name in the book of life seals the doom.

On "was cast into the lake of fire," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the final doom: such "was cast into the lake of fire" — the lost are cast into the lake of fire, the second death (v.14); the final, eternal perdition of all who are not Christ's; the solemn close of the judgment scene, before the new heaven and new earth. The verse displays the doom of those not in the book of life.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sentence — "whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire," the lost, not in the book of life, cast into the lake of fire (v.12; 13:8, Barnes, Robertson); "not found written in the book of life," the decisive register of the redeemed (Barnes, Gill); "was cast into the lake of fire," the final, eternal doom (v.14, Cyril). The verse displays the final doom. The verse marks the book: of life. The verse displays the test: not found written. The verse displays the doom: the lake of fire. The verse displays the finality: the second death. The sentence displays the lost cast into the lake of fire. The verse displays the names: absent. The verse displays the lost: condemned. The verse displays the fire: their doom. The doom of those not in the book of life displays the final sentence of the judgment: whoever is not found written in the book of life is cast into the lake of fire — the eternal perdition of all not Christ's, the second death. The verse displays the closed judgment, before the new heaven and new earth (ch.21).
